description: How violence is initiated, resolved, and survived: attack and defense resolution, positioning, wounds and recovery, and the action structures that pace a fight.

action_economy.description: How many things a combatant may do per turn and what structure governs spending them.

focuses.action_point_pools: Each activation grants a budget of action points spent freely across movement, attacks, and abilities, letting combatants trade mobility for offense (Zombicide's per-survivor actions, classic tactical RPGs).
focuses.multiple_action_penalty: Combatants may attempt several actions or attacks per round, but each additional one takes a cumulative penalty, making restraint a tactical choice (Savage Worlds multi-action penalty, Pathfinder 2e's multiple attack penalty).
focuses.three_action_system: Every turn grants exactly three interchangeable actions, with strikes, strides, and spell components all drawing from the same three (Pathfinder 2e).
focuses.minor_major_actions: Turns are divided into typed action slots — a major/standard action plus move and minor actions — each fillable only by actions of its type (D&D 4e standard/move/minor).
focuses.reactions_opportunity: Combatants hold limited reactions that interrupt other turns, most classically opportunity attacks against enemies leaving reach (D&D 5e reactions, Lancer overwatch).
focuses.held_actions: A combatant can delay or ready an action against a declared trigger, resolving it later in the round the moment the trigger occurs.
focuses.full_round_actions: The most powerful or complex options consume a combatant's entire turn, trading all other activity for one big effect (D&D 3.5 full-round actions).

focuses.initiative_boost: Implanted reflex hardware adds initiative dice or extra actions, letting augmented characters act earlier and more often than unaugmented ones (Shadowrun wired reflexes, Cyberpunk's Kerenzikov).

damage.description: How harm is quantified, inflicted, and represented once an attack succeeds.

focuses.armor_ablation: Armor carries a numeric rating that degrades as it absorbs hits, protecting less with each wound it stops until repaired (Cyberpunk 2020 SP ablation).
focuses.automatic_hit: Certain attacks skip the to-hit roll entirely and simply deal their effect, shifting play toward mitigation and resource trade-offs (magic-missile-style powers, scripted enemy attacks in many co-op board games).
focuses.contamination_cards: Taking harm or infection shuffles contamination cards into a player's deck or pool, clogging future draws and threatening worse outcomes when revealed or scanned (Nemesis).
focuses.critical_injury: Exceptional hits inflict rolled critical injuries with specific persistent effects that accumulate independently of ordinary damage (FFG Star Wars / Genesys critical injury table).
focuses.critical_wounds: Damage taken past a wound threshold becomes critical wounds — severe results threatening maiming or death rather than merely depleting a track (WFRP 4e critical wounds, Dark Heresy critical damage).
focuses.die_by_class: Weapon damage is set by the wielder's class or archetype rather than the weapon itself, so a fighter hits harder with anything at hand (an OSR-descended option in several retroclones).
focuses.heat_buildup: Attacks and systems generate heat on the acting unit; accumulated heat imposes penalties, forced shutdowns, or internal damage until vented (BattleTech, Lancer).
focuses.injury_tables: Serious hits trigger rolls on injury tables that specify concrete harms — broken limbs, bleeding, maiming — layered on top of or instead of numeric damage (WFRP critical charts, Twilight: 2000).
focuses.modifier_deck: Attack results are modified by flipping cards from a personal modifier deck instead of rolling dice, with curses and blessings tuning the deck over a campaign (Gloomhaven).
focuses.physical_wounds: Physical damage fills its own wound track separate from stun or mental harm, with the interplay between tracks deciding unconsciousness versus death (Shadowrun's physical and stun monitors).
focuses.ravage: Enemy forces damage a region and its defenders in a scheduled ravage step that players see coming and race to prevent or blunt (Spirit Island).
focuses.status_spectrums: Harm lands as named statuses with tier ratings along descriptive spectrums (e.g. burned-3) rather than hit points, with high tiers taking characters out (City of Mist).
focuses.structure_damage: Beneath the hit-point pool lies a structure or hull track; emptying HP damages structure and forces checks for catastrophic results like destroyed limbs or reactor breaches (Lancer, BattleTech internal structure).
focuses.tracks_aspects: Damage marks stress boxes and, when soaked, becomes consequence aspects — narrative facts like 'Broken Arm' that opponents can invoke against you (Fate).
focuses.wound_cards: Wounds are dealt as cards drawn from a wound deck, each carrying its own penalty, story detail, and recovery condition (Nemesis serious wounds, Sword & Sorcery).
focuses.wound_track: Damage fills a graduated track of health levels whose lower rungs impose worsening penalties on all actions (World of Darkness health levels).

defense.description: How combatants avoid, absorb, or cancel incoming attacks.

focuses.ambush_cards: Defenders may reveal hidden ambush cards when attacked, cancelling or punishing the assault before it resolves (Root).
focuses.block_phase: Combat includes a discrete block step in which defenders declare or allocate blocking values against incoming attacks before damage is assigned (Mage Knight).
focuses.combat_cards: Defense comes from playing combat cards out of a hand to modify, cancel, or redirect attacks, making card management part of survival (Blood Rage battle cards).
focuses.defend_cards: Dedicated defend or guard cards grant their block value only when played that turn, forcing a constant trade-off between defending and attacking (deckbuilders in the Slay the Spire mold, Undaunted).
focuses.dodge_pool: Defenders roll or allocate a dedicated dodge pool against each incoming attack, actively opposing strikes rather than relying on a static defense score (Shadowrun defense tests, older World of Darkness dodge splits).
focuses.electronic_warfare: Protection comes from jamming, spoofing, and sensor denial — electronic warfare actions degrade enemy targeting instead of stopping hits with armor (BattleTech ECM, Lancer e-war).
focuses.fortress_siege: Defense is structured around fortifications: walls, gates, and towers grant positional advantages that attackers must breach or scale across successive siege rounds (Stronghold).

focuses.ammo: Shots expend tracked ammunition, forcing reload actions and making ammo a tactical and logistical resource (round-by-round counts or usage-die abstractions).

focuses.lethality_rating: High-powered weapons carry a lethality percentage; on a hit the attacker rolls it, and success kills the target outright instead of rolling damage (Delta Green).

initiative.description: How the order of acting in combat is determined.

focuses.alternating: Sides alternate activating one unit or character at a time, so no faction resolves its whole force before the other responds (Malifaux, Lancer).
focuses.card_speed: Each round players commit ability or action cards whose printed initiative number sets the acting order (Gloomhaven).
focuses.conflict_card: A revealed conflict card frames each round's battle — its stakes and rewards — and combatants commit forces to it in turn (Dune: Imperium).
focuses.dexterity_based: Turn order is rolled or ranked from a Dexterity-style quickness attribute, fastest acting first (D&D-family initiative).
focuses.initiative_momentum: Initiative is held rather than rerolled: the side that wins an exchange keeps or seizes the tempo, acting first until momentum shifts (dueling systems like The Riddle of Steel).
focuses.narrative_flow: There is no initiative mechanic; the conversation of play decides who acts, with the GM cutting between characters as the fiction demands (PbtA games).
focuses.passes: Fast combatants receive multiple initiative passes, acting two or more times per round while slower ones act once (Shadowrun).
focuses.perception_based: Turn order derives from awareness or alertness rather than raw speed, so noticing the fight start first matters most.
focuses.token_bag_draw: Activation tokens for each side go into an opaque bag and are drawn one at a time, making turn order random and suspenseful every round (Bolt Action order dice).
focuses.weapon_speed: Weapon speed factors adjust when a combatant acts, letting daggers strike before greatswords regardless of the wielder's quickness (AD&D weapon speed, HackMaster).

maneuvers.description: Special combat moves beyond the basic attack.

focuses.grappling: Dedicated rules for grabbing and wrestling: opposed checks to seize, contested states like grabbed or pinned, and options to throw, restrain, or escape.
focuses.disarm: A maneuver to strike or twist a weapon out of an enemy's grip, typically an opposed roll that trades damage for the tactical swing of an unarmed foe.
focuses.shove_trip: Maneuvers that displace or topple rather than damage — pushing enemies out of position or knocking them prone to set up allies (D&D 5e shove, Pathfinder 2e trip).
focuses.called_shots: Attackers may target a specific body part or component at a to-hit penalty, trading accuracy for effects like blinding, crippling, or bypassing armor.
focuses.combat_stunts: Improvised flashy maneuvers adjudicated by a general stunt mechanic that converts risk or surplus successes into bonus effects (Exalted stunts, AGE system stunt points).
focuses.martial_techniques: Learnable named techniques expand a fighter's move list, each with prerequisites and specific effects (D&D 4e martial exploits, Pathfinder 2e feat chains).
focuses.stances: Fighters adopt mutually exclusive stances that reshape their options — aggressive, defensive, or stylistic modes switched as an action (Legend of the Five Rings stances, D&D 4e stances).
focuses.charge: A combined move-and-attack maneuver granting bonuses and drawbacks for closing at speed, usually with straight-line and minimum-distance requirements (D&D 3.5 charge, mounted lance rules).

focuses.action_points: Melee runs on an action-point budget where each swing, step, and guard spends from the same per-turn pool, letting fighters choose a few big efforts or many small ones.
focuses.attack_points: Fighters distribute a pool of attack points among their strikes in an exchange, splitting force across multiple blows or stacking it into one heavy hit.
focuses.battle_action: A whole fight, or a major chunk of it, resolves through one declared battle action or move, compressing blow-by-blow detail into a single decisive roll (Ironsworn's Battle move).
focuses.battle_card_tactics: Both sides secretly choose battle cards whose tactics — strength bonuses, casualties, retreats — resolve simultaneously on reveal (Kemet, A Game of Thrones: The Board Game house cards).
focuses.battle_wheels: Combatants secretly rotate a wheel or dial to select their maneuver for the exchange, with simultaneous reveal determining clashes and counters (dial-selection duel games).
focuses.cypher_combat: Combat under the Cypher System: players make all rolls against tiered difficulty targets, spending Effort from stat pools to lower them, while foes are flat difficulty ratings (Numenera, The Strange).
focuses.delve_hazards: Fights inside delves interleave monsters with environmental hazard draws, treating the dungeon itself as a combatant (encounter-and-hazard decks in dungeon-crawl board games).
focuses.dice_pools: Attacks roll a pool of dice built from attribute plus skill and count successes against a threshold, with more successes meaning cleaner or harder hits (Shadowrun, World of Darkness).
focuses.dice_rolls: Classic single-roll attack resolution: one die plus modifiers against a defense target number, hit or miss (the d20 attack roll and kin).
focuses.fight_action: Melee is one entry on a fixed action menu — declare the fight action, roll the governing skill, apply weapon damage (Year Zero Engine games like Forbidden Lands).
focuses.fight_scripting: Both fighters secretly script their next several actions — strike, feint, block — then reveal and cross-reference them exchange by exchange (Burning Wheel's Fight!).
focuses.fluid_conflict: Melee flows without a fixed turn structure; position, advantage, and narration shift continuously, with mechanics invoked only at decisive beats.
focuses.hit_locations: Each melee hit strikes a rolled or chosen body location with its own armor and consequences (RuneQuest).
focuses.jenga_tower_pull: Physical dexterity replaces dice: risky combat actions require pulls from a tumbling block tower, and toppling it means catastrophe (Dread).
focuses.military_comparison: Battles resolve by directly comparing the military strength totals on each side, higher value winning with little or no randomness (a staple of area-control and 4X board games).
focuses.military_tug_of_war: Combat pushes a shared marker along a tug-of-war track; committing forces shifts it toward your end, and reaching an end wins the struggle (Watergate, Twilight Struggle-style tracks).
focuses.momentum_based: Successful strikes generate momentum points that fuel extra damage, effects, and follow-ups, rewarding sustained pressure (2d20 games like Conan).
focuses.moves: Melee resolves through narrative moves with fixed outcome bands on hit, partial, and miss — trade harm, seize position — rather than attack-versus-defense math (PbtA's Hack and Slash).
focuses.narrative: Melee is adjudicated through fictional positioning and stakes-setting, with no tactical subsystem — just consequential storytelling punctuated by rolls.
focuses.nightmare_checks: Fighting horrors demands nightmare or terror checks alongside attacks, with failures feeding fear, madness, or the enemy's power (horror RPGs and dungeon-horror board games).
focuses.percentage_opposed: Attacker and defender both roll percentile skills and compare success levels to decide who lands the blow (RuneQuest and Mythras opposed rolls).
focuses.power_dial: Each combatant secretly dials in how much stored power to spend on the fight; reveals decide it, so bidding high wins but bluffing low saves resources (Scythe).
focuses.power_tags: Melee effectiveness comes from invoking thematic power tags on your character, each relevant tag adding to the roll (City of Mist).
focuses.sandworms_double_rewards: A conflict twist in which summoned sandworms double the victor's spoils, raising the stakes of committing to that round's battle (Dune: Imperium – Uprising).
focuses.scuffling: Close combat uses the Scuffling ability: spend points from a limited pool to boost a simple die roll, modeling investigators' finite fighting reserves (GUMSHOE).
focuses.shock_damage: Attacks deal shock — temporary, fast-healing trauma tracked separately from killing damage — so fights can end in knockouts rather than corpses (the One Roll Engine's Shock and Killing split).
focuses.speciation_survival: Conflict is evolutionary: species compete through adaptation and extinction eliminations rather than exchanged blows (Dominant Species).
focuses.strike_ranks: Actions in a round resolve in strike-rank order computed from size, dexterity, and weapon length, so reach and speed dictate tempo (RuneQuest).
focuses.tactical: Melee emphasizes grid positioning, forced movement, and ability synergy — combat as a tactical puzzle (D&D 4e, Gloomhaven).
focuses.tactical_mecha: Giant-machine close combat on a tactical map, where chassis, heat, and hardpoints shape melee options (Lancer, BattleTech physical attacks).
focuses.troops_deployed: Melee strength is a function of the troop units each side has in the contested area, with casualties removing units and eroding future strength (Kemet, Inis).
focuses.unarmed: Distinct rules for fists, holds, and brawling, typically with their own skill, damage type, and knockout-oriented outcomes.
focuses.weapon_expeditions: Martial power is assembled off-screen by sending expeditions to recover weapons and relics, which are then committed when forces finally clash.

morale.description: Whether combatants keep fighting, break, or yield.

focuses.morale_checks: NPCs and units test morale when bloodied, outnumbered, or leaderless; failure means faltering, retreating, or refusing to fight on (OSR morale scores, wargame morale tests).
focuses.rout_flee: Broken combatants rout under forced-flight rules governing where they run, whether they rally, and what free hits pursuers get (wargame rout moves).
focuses.surrender: Explicit rules for yielding: when enemies offer or accept surrender, and what mechanical weight captives, parley, and terms carry.
focuses.fear_and_terror: Frightening foes force fear tests before or during combat, with failure imposing penalties, paralysis, or flight regardless of wounds taken (WFRP fear and terror ratings).

movement.description: How combatants move about the battlefield.

focuses.dahan_counterattack: Native defenders strike back after being attacked in their lands, converting enemy aggression into automatic counter-damage that players can position around (Spirit Island's Dahan).
focuses.grid_based: Combat movement is counted in squares on a grid, with speeds, difficult terrain, and adjacency all measured in grid steps (D&D 3.5–5e battlemaps).
focuses.hex_grid: Movement uses a hexagonal grid, eliminating diagonal distortion and smoothing range and facing math (BattleTech, GURPS tactical combat).

positioning.description: How space, terrain, and relative location are represented and exploited.

focuses.zones_abstract: The battlefield divides into abstract zones rather than measured spaces — you are in a zone, adjacent to one, or far away, and movement is zone to zone (Fate).
focuses.square_grid: Combat plays out on a square grid where reach, areas of effect, and positioning are counted in squares (D&D battlemap play).
focuses.hex_grid: The battlefield is a hex map, giving uniform adjacency for facing, arcs, and true distances (BattleTech, Gloomhaven).
focuses.theater_of_mind: No map at all: positions live in shared imagination and GM adjudication, with mechanics tolerant of fuzzy geometry (D&D 5e theater of the mind).
focuses.cover_system: Formal cover states — half or full, soft or hard — grant defense bonuses or attack penalties, making terrain occupation a core tactic (D&D 5e cover, XCOM-style skirmish games).
focuses.flanking: Attacking from opposite sides or behind grants bonuses, encouraging pincer positioning and punishing isolated defenders (Pathfinder flanking, many miniatures games).
focuses.elevation: Height matters: high ground grants attack or range advantages and can block or enable line of sight (Heroscape, Lancer).
focuses.line_of_sight: Attacks require a traceable sight line checked against blocking terrain, from corner-to-corner tracing to true model's-eye view (miniatures wargames, Gloomhaven).
focuses.facing: Units have a facing, so attacks from flank or rear arcs gain bonuses and defenses like shields apply only frontally (BattleTech firing arcs, rank-and-flank wargames).
focuses.dudes_on_a_map: Skirmish combat emerges from area-control positioning: armies of pieces occupy regions and battles trigger where they collide (Risk lineage, Kemet, Blood Rage).

ranged.description: Resolution of attacks made at a distance.

focuses.fight_action: Shooting is a declared action from a fixed menu, resolved with one skill roll and weapon damage (Year Zero Engine ranged attacks).
focuses.friday_night_firefight: A gritty, lethal gunfight procedure emphasizing autofire, armor penetration, and fast death — realism over heroics (Cyberpunk 2020's Friday Night Firefight).
focuses.momentum_based: Ranged attacks generate and spend momentum for extra effects like burst control, called shots, and re-aims (2d20 system games).
focuses.narrative: Gunplay resolved through fiction and stakes, with position and cover treated as narrative facts rather than numeric modifiers.
focuses.range_and_cover: A scripted positioning duel where both sides maneuver to close, hold, or withdraw, success granting shots and cover advantages (Burning Wheel's Range and Cover).
focuses.range_brackets: Distances fall into brackets — short, medium, long, extreme — each with its own modifier or difficulty step instead of per-meter math (Traveller, FFG Star Wars range bands).
focuses.recoil_compensation: Automatic fire builds recoil penalties that strength, bracing, and gear offset, making weapon configuration matter for sustained fire (Shadowrun).
focuses.sensor_range: Targets must be detected on sensors before they can be engaged, making detection envelopes and lock-ons the real battlefield (space and mecha combat systems).
focuses.siege_phase: Ranged bombardment resolves in a dedicated siege step where artillery and engines fire before or apart from ordinary combat (wargames and area-control titles with siege rounds).
focuses.tactical: Ranged combat on a tactical map where line of sight, cover, and flanking determine shot quality (D&D 4e, Infinity).
focuses.tactical_shooting: A detailed shooting procedure modeling aim actions, fire modes, and shot placement step by step (Twilight: 2000, Phoenix Command).

restoration.description: How combatants recover resources and health between and after fights.

focuses.long_rest: An extended rest, typically overnight, restores most or all hit points and expended abilities, gating full recovery behind downtime (D&D 5e).
focuses.short_rest: A brief mid-adventure pause lets characters spend limited resources such as hit dice or encounter powers to partially recover between fights (D&D 4e/5e).

scale.description: Combat conducted above the individual scale: armies, fleets, sieges, and giant machines.

focuses.mass_combat: Armies fight as units with their own stats, morale, and commander rolls, resolving battles of hundreds without individual attacks (D&D's War Machine, Kingmaker-style mass combat).
focuses.unit_formations: Troops fight in formations whose shape and depth carry mechanical weight — shield walls, phalanxes, ranks and files (rank-and-flank wargames like Warhammer Fantasy Battles).
focuses.siege_warfare: Dedicated rules for besieging strongholds: siege engines, wall breaches, supply attrition, and assault timing.
focuses.naval_broadsides: Ship-of-the-line combat built on maneuvering for broadsides, the weather gauge, and raking fire (age-of-sail wargames).
focuses.aerial_dogfighting: Air-to-air combat as a maneuver duel — altitude, tailing position, and energy management decide who gets the shot (Wings of Glory, Crimson Skies).
focuses.mecha_scale: Combat at giant-robot scale, with conversion rules between mech-scale and personal-scale weapons and armor (BattleTech, Lancer).

space.description: Resolution of battles between spacecraft and fleets.

focuses.battle_resolution: Fleet engagements resolve through a structured battle procedure — rounds of dice per ship class with casualties assigned by their owner — abstracting maneuver away (Twilight Imperium space combat).
focuses.fleet_formations: How a fleet is composed and arranged grants combat effects: capital ships screened by escorts, formations trading firepower for protection.

status.description: Temporary conditions and ongoing effects applied to combatants.

focuses.conditions_tracking: A standardized list of conditions — prone, stunned, frightened — with precise rules text, applied and removed by attacks and abilities (D&D 5e, Pathfinder 2e conditions).
focuses.knockback: Hits physically displace targets, slamming them into walls, hazards, or off ledges for additional effect (Lancer knockback, HeroClix).
focuses.stun_daze: Effects that deny actions — stunned, dazed, slowed — trading raw damage for action-economy denial (Pathfinder 2e stunned value, D&D 4e daze).
focuses.bleeding: Wounds inflict ongoing bleed damage each round until staunched, turning untreated injuries into a ticking clock (Pathfinder 2e persistent damage).
focuses.poison_tracking: Poisons run on their own procedure of onset times, staged effects, and repeated saves rather than instant damage (Pathfinder 2e poison stages).

wounds.description: The underlying model of injury: what damage does to a body and how it is represented.

focuses.hit_points: Harm depletes a numeric hit-point pool with no mechanical effect until it empties — damage as attrition rather than injury (D&D and most of its descendants).
focuses.wound_levels: Characters have a short ladder of discrete wound states rather than a large pool, each absorbed hit stepping them toward incapacitation (Savage Worlds wounds).
focuses.injury_tables: The wound model itself is table-driven: rather than tracking points, each serious hit rolls on a chart whose entries are the character's actual wounds (Rolemaster criticals and other old-school crit charts).
focuses.death_spiral: Injuries impose penalties that make further injuries likelier, so the wounded slide toward defeat — an accepted or deliberate design of grim realism (World of Darkness wound penalties).
focuses.lingering_injuries: Serious harm leaves persistent injuries that outlast the fight and demand downtime, treatment, or permanent adaptation (Blades in the Dark harm, D&D 5e's optional lingering injuries).
focuses.massive_damage: A single hit past a threshold triggers special rules — instant-death saves or trauma — regardless of hit points remaining (D&D 3.5 massive damage).
focuses.armor_as_soak: Armor subtracts from incoming damage rather than making the wearer harder to hit, turning wounds into bruises (RuneQuest armor points, Shadowrun soak).
focuses.ablative_armor: Armor acts as an expendable buffer pool depleted before flesh, restored by repair rather than healing (shield and armor pools in sci-fi and mecha games).
focuses.hit_locations: Damage and armor are tracked per body location, each with its own points and specific consequences when disabled (RuneQuest limb hit points).
